General.text: My DNA is against the BJP: Kalvakuntla Kavitha

The former MP and daughter of K. Chandrashekar Rao says there is no question of her joining hands with the party, adding that if the BRS had believed her, they would not have suspended her.Ayesha  Minhaz
The collapse of our moral universe
We are today seeing the construction of a world order in which injustice and violence are quite openly praised and where domination and exclusion are normalised.Rajeev Bhargava
Asymmetry, the elephant in the room

The decision to keep in abeyance the UGC rules on caste discrimination ignores the fact that actors with greater social capital, procedural familiarity, and institutional backing are better positioned to mobilise grievance processes strategically.Anirban Ghatak
The dream warriors
The Tiny Farm Lab in Rishikesh offers a glimpse of life in the hill sand shows how a group of dreamers can make choices, small and big, in the hope of making a difference.Rebecca Mathai
Motorcycle diaries of a reluctant traveller
All I know is that as long as I am able, I will occasionally get my bike ready for the highway and then figure out if I am still ready for life on the highway.Eshwar  Sundaresan
White sheets and white lies
Luxury hotels protect you from visible dirt and disorder and also give you the luxury of not knowing too much or feeling too much.Annie Zaidi
Editor’s Note
Vaishna Roy
The RSS bites deep into the society: Tanika Sarkar
The historian says the RSS works silently, out of sight, making its reach hard to gauge.Abhish  K. Bose
Music plays a huge role in articulating social problems: Sumangala Damodaran
A singer, economist, and archivist of dissent, Damodaran says the archive can be a battleground and a rehearsal for the future.Swapna Majumdar
Aren’t we all autistic?
When society rewards sameness and calls it inclusion, the real disorder might be in how it pathologises anyone who senses the world differently.Meera  Rao
Human-wildlife conflict can reduce only if community rights are recognised: C.R. Bijoy
Despite the FRA, forest departments still control forests, not forest-dwellers and gram sabhas, says the researcher of natural resource conflicts.Mridula  Chari
Sooner or later there will be a reaction: Tariq Ali
Israel, thinking itself unbeatable, could find its very existence under siege over the next 10, 15, or 20 years, says the public intellectual.Vaishna Roy
Tourism and climate change are changing Uttarakhand forever
During Askot Arakot Abhiyan, we witnessed at close quarters the damage caused by heedless development in the fragile Himalayan terrain.Umesh  Pant
Sunil Gupta retrospective: Desire, identity, and the weight of the image
Indian-Canadian gay photographer Sunil Gupta’s portraits emerge from somewhere between assertion and narcissism, archiving both identity and the self.Prathyush Parasuraman
‘The Dystopian Times’ by Appupen
Readers respond to Frontline’s coverage.
In the lanes of Amritsar’s Guru Bazar, a gold rush like no other
A community of sweepers separate the gold particles from the dust and sell it back to the jewellers.Shivam  Khanna

Readers respond to Frontline’s coverage.

Readers respond to Frontline’s coverage.
Ways of unseeing: The art of overanalysing art
A kind of criticism dwells in the distance created between art and meaning. Why is the obvious so suspect? Why is the surface not nourishing enough?Prathyush Parasuraman
Readers respond to Frontline’s coverage.
Readers respond to Frontline’s coverage.
‘Archiving’ the 2020-21 farmers’ protest
Two recent documentaries, Trolley Times and Farming the Revolution, stand as counter-archives to what the state refuses to show.Prathyush Parasuraman
Readers respond to Frontline’s coverage.
Dune 2: Landscape as protagonist
Can land be that anchoring, propulsive presence through which the art makes itself known?Prathyush Parasuraman
Navigating progressive rhetoric at the Berlinale 2024
Despite its reputation, this year’s edition suggests that the festival is not the most ahead politically but one that is least behind.Prathyush Parasuraman

Polity.text: The disabled and the damned
How UP’s half encounters punish first, judge later.Ashutosh Sharma
Will the VBSA Bill actually regulate higher education, or just fund its decline?

The government’s new “light but tight” education commission centralises control while offloading funding. This could force universities to hike fees, narrowing access and imposing a political curriculum under the guise of reform.T.K. Rajalakshmi
Honey, I healed my chakras!
A year of mindful wellness regimes has left me smelling like a forest, looking like a walking ICU, and firmly headed towards moksha.Kalpish Ratna
Chhattisgarh’s unquiet peace
Even as the State celebrates its “victory” over naxalism, extractive development now threatens Adivasi communities more than the gunfire ever did.Ashutosh Sharma
Uttarakhand: An unholy mess
It was created to ensure the hills got a better deal, but they continue to be neglected even as ecologically unsound tourism projects get priority.Soni Mishra
Make smaller States stronger
Carving out smaller States alone does not guarantee better outcomes if it is not accompanied by greater devolution of powers.Nilakantan  R.S.
Editor’s note: Where is the development?
The three States carved out 25 years ago stand as monuments not to ‘double-engine’ growth, but to ecological wreckage and failed development.Vaishna Roy
Belling the RSS cat
Priyank Kharge’s uncomfortable questions about the RSS have turned the spotlight on an organisation that has long evaded regulatory scrutiny.Vikhar Ahmed Sayeed
Editor’s note: A masterclass in ‘level’ playing fields
A pre-set game decided Bihar’s match. The ECI’s biased rulings, a silenced media, and a flood of cash have changed the rules of democracy.Vaishna Roy
The horse that outran the field
The NDA’s sweeping victory rested on a nine-point vote share lead and an exceptional mobilisation of women voters.Sanjay  Kumar
Editor’s note: The great Bihar sprint
Another political race begins, marked by fanfare, cash, caste, and missing voters—a contest where democracy seems to limp behind.Vaishna Roy
One hundred years of neglect
Hindutva is not Hinduism
Can Akhand Bharat be reimagined?
India’s progress depends on fostering Hindu–Muslim harmony and healing. Does the RSS have the imagination to shed its exclusionary ideas?Sudheendra Kulkarni
No Muslim, no Hindutva
Will the RSS lose control of its most successful creation?
Is Satyameva Jayate still India’s truth?
The opposition’s allegation of vote chori is grave. Fair elections manifest truth in democracies; their absence compromises any democracy.V Kumari Sunitha ,Bins Sebastian
Votes count, but trust counts more
For the ECI to restore the credibility and sanctity of elections, it must ensure transparency at every stage—from voter lists to the final results.Poonam Agarwal
The ECI’s credibility collapse
The Election Commission’s opacity and its willingness to bend rules in favour of the ruling dispensation strike at the root of constitutional democracy.Parakala Prabhakar
Editor’s Note: Truth on the ballot
The republic’s promise rests on honest rolls and fair counts. Without them, democracy is a sham.Vaishna Roy
Muslim minority educational institutions and the unfinished promise of Article 30
Constitutional safeguards keep them accessible and safe, yet state neglect and rising majoritarianism turn the promise into a cycle of exclusion.Hem Borker
India’s Sputnik moment
NIPUN Bharat has lifted literacy and numeracy in early grades, with rural, State schools—long derided— now showing better results than private ones.Anustup Nayak,Harshit Pai
India’s elite have grown comfortable with inequality: Surinder Jodhka
The sociologist unpacks how caste persists in India’s education system and why equality of opportunity remains distant.Vishal Vasanthakumar
Give the child a fish
The Right to Education Act, 2009, remains the world’s boldest attempt at affirmative action. Yet amendments in a few States threaten to unravel it.Tarun Cherukuri
Adani’s great land grab in Maharashtra leaves farmers fuming
A group company appears to have bypassed norms and commitments that would have benefited those who owned the land.Amey Tirodkar ,Anand Mangnale
Will Hindi unite the Thackerays?
Yes, if Fadnavis keeps giving them reasons to share a stage after 19 years to protect Marathi from BJP’s language experiments.Amey Tirodkar
In Bihar, caste holds steady even as alliances shift
The RJD, JD(U), BJP, and Congress try adding new caste groups to their base, but their vision ends at the edge of old social formulas.Anand Mishra
Can Prashant Kishor do a Kejriwal in Bihar?
Staying away from caste and focusing on education, employment, and empowerment is the message the political strategist-politician is banking on.Anand Mishra
Lalu and Nitish carry their legacies to the edge
Born of a socialist moment, they remade Bihar in their own image. This battle may be their last; the politics they built won’t outlive them unchanged.Soni Mishra
Assam enters the danger zone
Elections are months away, but the groundwork is laid: define the outsider, arm the insider, and let the chaos do the rest.Sushanta Talukdar
In Assam, ‘indigenous’ means many things—until it means Muslim
How the RSS, by astutely marrying Assamese nationalism with Hindutva, turned Muslim immigrants into the perfect outsider.Greeshma  Kuthar
Legal yet lawless: Assam’s new wave of deportations follows a long tradition of expulsion politics
And that is now culminating in the normalisation of a culture of anti-minority violence.Angshuman  Choudhury
Editor’s Note: The Assam template
Target a community, arm the rest, and call it security. If it worked in Manipur—for a while—why not try it next door?Vaishna Roy
Editor’s Note: Dialogue is the only long-term answer
Amid missiles and chest-thumping, dialogue may sound dull. But it’s the only tool left that doesn’t blow up—literally. Time to use it, not mock it.Vaishna Roy
True federalism demands equitable resource distribution, not selective development
For federalism to work, resources must be fairly shared. States like Bihar and Odisha must receive the same support as Gujarat and Maharashtra.Sudhakar Singh ,Sarish  Tripathi
Those who choose guns become enemies of the nation: Basant K. Ponwar
Constitution forbids taking up arms against the state. Grievances must be addressed democratically, not through violence, says the retired Brigadier.Ashutosh Sharma
Gadchiroli: A different minefield
Behind Maharashtra’s ‘development and ambush’ strategy lies a push to exploit tribal lands, sacrificing indigenous livelihoods for mining interests.Amey Tirodkar
Prioritise a timeline to eliminate poverty and exploitation: Ramesh Badranna
Former Maoist guerrilla commander says the government’s militarised approach is doomed to fail unless it addresses the root causes behind naxalism.Ashutosh Sharma
The Adivasis bear the brunt: Bela Bhatia
Bastar-based lawyer says tribal communities of the region have been living under the shadow of the gun for close to two decades now.Ashutosh Sharma
‘Many naxalites are willing to join the mainstream’: Noor Sridhar
Former leader of the People’s War and CPI (Maoist) says respectful reintegration, not repression, is key to ending Naxal violence.Vikhar Ahmed Sayeed
Editor’s Note: The dogged determination of Arvind Kejriwal
With the odds stacked against him, can the AAP chief come back as Chief Minister of Delhi for a fourth term?Vaishna Roy
The elected government in Delhi cannot transfer even a clerk: P.D.T. Achary
Former Lok Sabha Secretary General says the Centre’s actions have hobbled the Delhi government and made it non-functional.Anand Mishra
Kejriwal is a spent force: Baijayant Panda
BJP’s Delhi election in-charge says Kejriwal can no longer claim to be “staunchly honest” due to corruption charges; he has run Delhi into its first fiscal deficit.Soni Mishra
All is not well
Public health is the stepchild of policymaking in India, where private care gets all the attention. The state must see it as a right, not a business.Vandana Prasad
The blackboard crisis
Between Central control and State autonomy, India’s education system searches for balance as wider social divisions deepen its struggle for direction.Krishna Kumar
Agriculture in the age of inequality
The corporate hijack of agriculture, achieved by predatory commercialisation of the countryside, is India’s actual agrarian crisis.P. Sainath
An unfraternal democracy
While neighbours fell to majoritarianism, India stayed pluralist, if imperfectly. What helped it resist so long, and why did it finally give in?Mukul Kesavan
Recalibrating the moral compass
There is a gulf between the Constitution’s values and those of the present government, undermining the very idea of India. But this, too, shall pass.Indira Jaising
BJP’s agenda is to defame Muslims: Syed Naseer Hussain
MP and member of the JPC says the BJP wants to desecularise India.Vikhar Ahmed Sayeed
Propriety, not just property: The Waqf debate
Since the impacts of the amendments will go far beyond legal changes, the government must balance reforms with the rights of the Muslim community.Shaikh  Mujibur Rehman
Right move, wrong motive? Waqf Bill promises better management but delivers state control
The changes would remove key protections for waqf properties, centralising control under government officials rather than community representatives.P.S. Munawar Hussain
Who really benefits from the Waqf Amendment Bill?
While some provisions attempt to address corruption and mismanagement, the Bill contradicts BJP government’s stated “one nation, one law” principle.Faizan  Mustafa
Women of the Hindutva family
How the BJP uses religious symbolism and welfare schemes to win women voters.Neera Chandhoke
The myth of the ‘women vote bank’
The narrative of women as mere beneficiaries (labharthis) undermines their political agency and ignores the paradoxical realities of their lives.Rajeshwari  Deshpande
Welfare schemes win female voters but fail to boost women in politics
Recent elections showed parties shower women voters with cash schemes and promises, but women remain conspicuously absent from the halls of power.Suhrid Sankar Chattopadhyay
Women voters: New political powerhouse?
Women-specific welfare schemes now help parties win elections. Yet whether women vote as a unified group or follow traditional loyalties is unclear.Ashish  Ranjan
BJP falls back on Hindutva after election setbacks
Muslims are projected as enemies of OBCs and tribal people ahead of the next ballot box round.Anand Mishra
Editor’s Note: Maharashtra is in some serious trouble today
Its infrastructure is a mess, its record of farmer suicides is frightening, and, most worryingly, its sociopolitical fabric is being rent apart.Vaishna Roy
Arambai Tenggol: How a Meitei ‘sociocultural organisation’ became an armed-to-the-teeth militia
The Manipur conflict has many moving parts, but Arambai Tenggol has emerged as a key driving force that continues to shape and fuel it.Angshuman  Choudhury
Eyes wide shut: How the state turns away from Manipur’s realities
The government’s demonisation of the Kuki-Zo and support for radical Meitei groups has driven a permanent wedge between the two communities.Greeshma  Kuthar
India’s employment crisis: Jobless growth, threat of automation, and impact of climate change on productivity
Employment growth has stagnated at 2 per cent for two decades. Without reaching 4-5 per cent, absorbing surplus labour will be impossible.Sujoy  Chakravarty
India is haunted by an unprecedented economic deprivation
The situation, marked by widespread unemployment and inequality, calls for policy that improves the lives of the working people while taxing the rich.Prabhat Patnaik
One nation, one election: A long and winding poll
The bigger malaise hidden here is the corrosion of a vital independent institution such as the Election Commission.Nissim Mannathukkaren
Editor’s Note: Electoral bonds, and our tumbledown democracy
The BJP exudes an almost contemptuous confidence that no revelations can derail its ultimate victory. Where does this confidence come from?Vaishna Roy
Power to the people: India’s democracy needs a subaltern revival
For democracy to function, the masses must feel invested in it. A subaltern strategy thus could be a potent tool to counter the BJP’s hegemony.Asim  Ali
‘I don’t get rattled easily’: Sukhvinder Singh Sukhu
The Chief Minister of Himachal Pradesh says the BJP cultivated the six rebel MLAs to cross-vote in the Rajya Sabha election.T.K. Rajalakshmi
The murky political goings-on in Himachal Pradesh
Following the recent rebellion of six MLAs, the Congress finds itself on shaky ground in the only State it now runs in north India.T.K. Rajalakshmi

Social Issues.text: Why women in mathematics still need a room of their own

This exhibition pairs portraits of women mathematicians and physicists from around the world with their personal stories, with the aim of inspiring young women, who remain under-represented in the sciences.Uma  Mahadevan-Dasgupta
When justice is branded as ‘divisive’
The Supreme Court’s stay on the UGC regulations is a moral blunder and constitutional betrayal.Ankush Pal,Dipankar Kamble
UGC equity rules are a carefully curated political gesture: Anand Teltumbde

The academic and rights activist on the politics behind the new rules, the staged backlash from dominant castes, and why the focus on rankings and “global benchmarks” reproduces structural disadvantages for scholars from marginalised communities.Amey Tirodkar
First among unequals
The last-mile delivery of the constitutional right to equality has remained unfulfilled since Independence, especially in India’s educational institutions.Greeshma  Kuthar
Editor’s note
Vaishna Roy
Time for humans to reboot
After losing to AI the exclusive claim to language, humans must now find value in a world increasingly populated by machines that “think” and “speak”.Anil  Menon
Love in the time of jomo
Everyone is embracing digital culture. Ironically, the more technology is reducing distances, the more it is reducing closeness too.Akhila  Ramnarayan
How idealism became pragmatism
Every movement begins with a dream and ends with a negotiation. The Self-Respect Movement’s political afterlife proves no cause stays pure.B. Jeyamohan
They broke the chains, not the ceiling
While the women leaders of the Self-Respect Movement transformed society in profound ways, full empowerment of women remains far from fully achieved.A Arulmozhi
The century of self-respect
A hundred years later, the Self-Respect Movement’s ideals of equality, women’s liberation, and the annihilation of caste are finding new geographies.Karthick  Ram Manoharan
Editor’s note: The other freedom fight
Periyar knew self-rule without self-respect meant servitude in disguise. A hundred years later, his point still stands—loudly.Vaishna Roy
Gaza, and the unbearable safety of watching
Every cry for food in Gaza echoes through our curated despair. A child’s hunger indicts our comforts, our screens, our art, our failure to act.Shaoni  Sarkar
Carl Schmitt was right: The sovereign decides the exception
His idea of sovereignty as the power to declare exceptions explains today’s political crises better than the fading certainties of liberal theory.Karthick  Ram Manoharan
Ban on cannabis is harming millions; it must be lifted: Viki Vaurora
The activist argues for complete legalisation, pointing to cannabis’s therapeutic properties, industrial applications, and historical significance.Divya  Gandhi
Punjab’s drug crisis calls for natural intoxicant reform
Congress leader Dharamvira Gandhi proposes decriminalisation of oppium, cannabis to combat drug menace.Ashutosh Sharma
A new leaf: Unlocking cannabis’ therapeutic value
CSIR-IIIM Jammu leads groundbreaking study on medical cannabis, awaits crucial permits.Ashutosh Sharma
Will Himachal’s cannabis resolution open a Pandora’s box?
Poor monitoring, existing drug problems, and the State’s inability to control THC levels in hemp can turn legal cultivation into public health crisis.T.K. Rajalakshmi
The steady immiseration of labour
By the end of the 1990s, both capital and a willing state had eviscerated the labour movement. The death blow will be dealt in the 2020s.Sudha Bharadwaj
Owners’ pride: Kolkata’s Trincas
How a century-old Park Street icon became the city’s unlikely LGBTQIA+ haven.SANDIP  ROY
Poppies in Flanders
The fallen soldiers of the First World War are a living presence at the memorial in Ypres, Belgium, dedicated to their valour.Aditya  Sondhi
On love and other demons
What is it that we talk about when we talk about love?Kartik  Chauhan
Words on a plate
The menu boasts no dish names but will serve up similes and metaphors, oxymorons and syllogism, with metonymy that might pass for irony.Kalpish Ratna

International Relations.text: Editor’s note
Vaishna Roy
Beyond docility or defiance: India’s new post-Trump reality
Confronting an imploding global order, India’s response must be measured multi-alignment. The era of relying on a single, capricious power is over.Paul M. McGarr
Editor’s Note
Vaishna Roy
Power as ultimate truth

US policy under Donald Trump 2.0 marks a shift from strategic altruism to strategic impatience; middle powers like India will need sharper signalling, faster decision-making, and deeper investment in resilience.Nirupama  Rao
Bangladesh’s moment of reckoning
Khaleda Zia’s death makes way for political realignment. Whether this will help break Bangladesh’s cycle of retributive violence is an open question.Manash  Firaq Bhattacharjee
Netanyahu’s military successes come at a dangerous cost
Israel’s unchecked aggression has deepened the region’s fault lines—and its own vulnerabilities.Bashir Ali  Abbas
Israel’s ‘total war’ has wrecked the Axis of Resistance but not its logic
Iran and allied groups will reconfigure around shared ideology and informal economies, and lasting peace demands a viable Palestinian state.Anwar Alam
Israel’s 12-day war leaves Iran on a security-first path
The war deepened Iran’s isolation but revived its nationalist core, accelerating a shift from revolutionary idealism to strategic pragmatism.Deepika Saraswat
Israel-Hamas war: A geopolitical earthquake that redefined West Asia and global politics
It has forced Israel back into dependence on the U.S., affected Iran, Lebanon, Syria, and Türkiye, and even expanded China’s role in the region.Iftikhar Gilani
Editor’s Note: The West’s bottomless greed created Gaza and Ukraine crises
These conflicts strip Western liberal democracy—Fukuyama’s proclaimed “final form of human government”—of its pretence, exposing a miserable failure.Vaishna Roy
Democracy, memory, and cultural confidence in South Korea
South Korea’s approach to remembering and respecting history provides India with a template as it grapples with a lack of self-confidence.Lawrence  Surendra
India’s strategic agility will define its US ties in Trump’s second act
The US and India must make ties irreversible, but Trump’s tendency to turn on allies means New Delhi faces a tough balancing act ahead.Jayant  Prasad
Will Trump break world’s faith in US dollar?
Global markets brace for impact as Trump’s economic threats and spending plans risk undermining international confidence in America’s currency.Radhika  Desai
Editor’s Note: Trump’s second coming will reset world’s moral compass
With his return as US President, we are witnessing in real time the takeover of governance by Big Capital.Vaishna Roy
Trump’s brand of diplomacy can push the world to the edge
The potential for global unrest is likely to be higher, given Trump’s volatility, transactional approach, and unorthodox diplomatic dealings.Ambreen  Yousuf
Enter the age of corporate-feudal warlords
Trump’s second term will only reinforce the processes begun by Biden’s administration to dismantle international law, democracy, and multilateralism.Tabish Khair
Iran’s anti-Israel stance has evolved from a purely ideological position into a sophisticated political instrument
Tehran now faces a complex balancing act: leading the fight against Israel, and avoiding an all-out war, while managing domestic unrest.Bashir Ali  Abbas
Anura Dissanayake: The outsider with a difference
Dissanayake may not come across as a friend of Tamils, but being the practical politician that he is, he may still have some surprises.R.K. Radhakrishnan
The Indus Waters Treaty faces a perfect storm
Having endured all ebbs and flows in the India-Pakistan relationship so far, the IWT must now find a way out of the existing politico-legal quagmire.Bashir Ali  Abbas
Palestinian Authority’s declining influence portends more trouble for Gaza
This is compounded by US financial cuts and internal challenges of corruption, limited autonomy, and a shattered political structure.Shubhda  Chaudhary
Is Pakistan undergoing a reset in army-civilian ties?
As the army realises the folly of its political interventions, Pakistan hopes for a tentative military-civilian equilibrium to strengthen democracy.Kesava Menon
Indian Ocean: By the numbers
The Indian Ocean Region is at the centre of global commerce.Talmiz  Ahmad
India’s balancing act: Navigating the evolving geopolitics of the Indian Ocean and the Indo-Pacific
As the Gaza war spills into Red Sea impacting trade and security, India navigates complex alliances and its own strategic interests in Indian Ocean.Talmiz  Ahmad

History & Culture.text: New books on the shelves
Stories set in the intimate theatres of everyday life, essays on India’s teeming urban homeless, and more.
The great history divide

In Speaking of History, historian Romila Thapar and history enthusiast Namit Arora stage a conversation on caste, method, and early India, but the exchange also reveals the limits of pop history when it brushes against serious scholarship.R.  Mahalakshmi
New books on the shelves
A novel by the Tamil writer Salma in translation, the feminist icon Gisele Pelicot’s unwavering testament to survival and hope, and much more.
New books on the shelves
A classic Assamese novel in translation, an unapologetically desi anthology on love and sex in India, and more.
The Kushans of Kashmir
The recent discovery of the ruins of an ancient Buddhist stupa in Baramulla suggests that Kashmir was probably a key outpost for the ancient Kushans.Shakir Mir
The disappearing art of the ‘kuchh gaa ke sunao’ moment

That awkward picnic request for a song once crafted cinematic gems like ‘Main kaun sa geet sunaoon’. Today’s reels and shorts have killed the courtesy, and the clever, situational music it inspired.Hansda Sowvendra Shekhar
New books on the shelves
A financial thriller, history of India’s national symbols, and much more.
The moon pond
Translated from the Tamil by V. RamakrishnanD.  Ravikumar
A mind without fear
J.M. Kumarappa’s 1928 study of Tagore offers a vital reflection on education, addressing modern challenges through a vision of child-centred learning.Divya Kannan
New books on the shelves
A murder mystery about wronged women, an investigation into the militant Buddhism that is transforming Asia, and more.
Divine fusion
A collaboration between a Kuchipudi dancer and a Pichwai artist amalgamates the two art forms, enriching our understanding of both in unusual ways.Devina  Dutt
Trek to nowhere
Granting any attention to this brick of a crime thriller by Robert Galbraith, aka J.K. Rowling, feels like a criminal waste of time and energy.Aditya Sinha
Beautiful and broken
This insider’s view of the art world is at once a rollicking read and a serious commentary on the commercialisation of the art space.Ritika  Kochhar
A zeitgeist of bookmaking
Tyler Williams’ literary historiography is an engaging exploration of the materiality of medieval desh bhasha manuscripts and the evolution of Hindi.Varsha  Tiwary
A mother’s sudden death leaves two daughters as invisible “residue” in a vast Kerala home.Catherine  Thankamma
New books on the shelves
A novel about lesbian love set in Cameroon, a book of essays on India’s “extremely online” Gen Z, and much more.
Zombie economics
This book purports to analyse post-Independence India’s economy and social challenges but serves as an elitist justification of the status quo.Ashoka Mody
New books on the shelves
A supernatural romantic thriller, the biography of a mighty river, and much more.
Wrapped in melancholy
Diversity and plurality are the motto in this anthology of persistently inward-looking graphic narratives.Bharath  Murthy
Maguni’s bullock cart
Translated from Odia by Leelawati Mohapatra, Paul St-Pierre, and K.K. MohapatraGodavarish  Mahapatra
New books on the shelves
A satirical feminist folktale, a collection of essays to commemorate the centenary of Indian cinema’s unsung genius, and much more.
How Hindutva comes for Adivasis
Choubey’s study reveals how the VKA uses service delivery to advance a larger agenda: dissolving tribal identity into undifferentiated Hindu majority.Sharmila Purkayastha
New books on the shelves
A magical realist novel about a river with political acumen, a volume that redefines Tamil Studies, and much more.
Fish all the way
Translated from Telugu by D.V. SubhashriMohammed  Khadeer Babu
Dissent without borders
Sugata Srinivasaraju’s book shows how a forgotten diaspora network, armed with Gandhian ideals and stubborn moral clarity, fought Emergency from afar.Karthick  Ram Manoharan
New books on the shelves
A novel set in COVID times, a biography of M.S. Swaminathan and much more.
A.B. Vajpayee: A charismatic RSS ideologue
This engaging biography gives a colourful account of the multilayered relationships within the Sangh Parivar and deals with a turbulent time in India.S. Irfan  Habib
New books on the shelves
A fresh translation of the Tamil writer Salma’s first novel, a richly detailed study of feminist street theatre in India, and much more.
New books on the shelves
An essential introduction to Nagaland through its fiction, the story of actor-director Aparna Sen’s life in cinema, and much more.
The Cambridge six
Engerman’s Apostles of Development traces how six South Asian economists carried Cambridge ideas into the tangled politics of growth and inequality.Sukumar  Muralidharan
Extreme wealth is toxic
In Limitarianism, Ingrid Robeyns makes the case for limits on fortunes that poison society, politics, and the planet.Jean  Drèze
New books on the shelves
A controversial Hindi novel exploring queer identity now in English translation, the stories of 18 Muslim women MPs, and much more.
New books on the shelves
The fourth book in a detective novel series set in 1920s Bangalore, the memoirs of a physician who fought to prevent Partition, and more.
Bankers of the East
How a Tamil business community dominated 19th-century South-East Asian commerce before colonialism and World Wars cemented its decline.Amol  Agrawal
New books on the shelves
A novel that harks back to Kerala’s naxalite movement, a book on the invisible lives that enable our digital ease, and more.
Translated from the Hindi by Nirya Misra.Siyaram Sharan  Gupta
New books on the shelves
The quest for the mythical river Saraswati, the legacy of the 1955 Bandung Conference on non-alignment, and much more.
New books on the shelves
The sequel to Rudyard Kipling’s Kim, the untold story of an Indian uprising against the British much before the Revolt of 1857, and more.
New books on the shelves
A translated novel reimagining the life of Jesus Christ, an account of the Gandhian movement, and much more.
The showman and his songs
However far Hindi cinema has moved from its older idioms, Raj Kapoor’s song performances remain fresh and accessible to those who seek them out.Jai Arjun Singh
Acting innocent
In Raj Kapoor’s later cinema, innocence is no longer organic—it is a stylised deflection, a mask worn after the fall, not before it.Prathyush Parasuraman
Song of the vagabond heart
A fine musician himself, Raj Kapoor introduced lyricist Shailendra and composer duo Shankar-Jaikishan, redefining Hindi cinema and their careers.Juhi Saklani
The showman who accidentally documented India’s soul
If one can read a country through its films, Raj Kapoor’s cinema mapped India’s drift from post-Independence optimism to wealth-obsessed conformity.C.S.  Venkiteswaran
You can’t replicate Raj Kapoor today: Nasreen Munni Kabir
The writer and documentary filmmaker explores Raj Kapoor’s cinema, his political vision, and timeless collaborations with Abbas and Shailendra.Varrun  Sukhraj
The poor little rich man
Raj Kapoor’s films dressed up socialism for the big screen—but the class line was clear: the elite still got the starring roles.M.K. Raghavendra
New books on the shelves
A thriller set in Delhi’s posh Panchsheel area, biography of Raghunath Dhondo Karve, personal narratives of three historians, and much more.
What if the real builders of Indian civilisation never got to write the story?
Kancha Ilaiah’s The Shudra Rebellion rewrites history from below, where spades built cities and obedience was not a choice.Cynthia Stephen
Life in the double-edged metropolis
A nuanced investigation into Bengaluru’s “speculative urbanism” and how citizens’ innovation and survival hold the city together.Srikar  Raghavan
New books on the shelves
Vintage Kannada short fiction in translation, a candid meditation on the cruel finality of death, and much more.
A Passage to India: A novel unchanged, yet forever evolving
A hundred years on, E.M. Forster’s A Passage to India endures with its honesty and affection, serving as a mirror in which readers can see themselves.Kalpish Ratna
Kabir, customised
What emerges through Anand’s translation of the 15th century poet-saint’s verses is his own personal Kabir, mediated through Ambedkar and the Buddha.Sanjay  Kumar
New books on the shelves
An American novel described as a study of the unholy catastrophe of marriage, a timely primer on delimitation and much more.
Amitav Ghosh on the age of monsters
In Wild Fictions, Ghosh links climate, capitalism, and migration, with side notes on vests, spice routes, and why tigers are not always good news.Neha Sinha
New books on the shelves
A blackly comic Booker Prize-longlisted novel, a history of a tree-hugging people, and much more.
New books on the shelves
The biggest and most ambitious collection of Indian short fiction, a tribute to the “sound of India”, and many more.
New books on the shelves
A novel about the lives of the world’s richest 1 per cent, a book on the complicated history of Palestinian commemoration, and many more.
Caste, violence and a September afternoon in Bundelkhand
Priyadarshini Vijaisri’s Essays on Violence examines the cultural, religious, and political origins of caste violence in India.Moggallan  Bharti
The price of moral absolution: An extract from The World After Gaza by Pankaj Mishra
Mishra on the hypocrisies of liberal democracies, the resurgence of ethno-nationalism, and the dangerous alignments shaping the post-Gaza world.Pankaj Mishra
New books on the shelves
An allegorical work by a Hindi literary great, a novel exploring Delhi’s dark underbelly, a book on India’s torch-bearers of progressivism, and more.
Editor’s Note: Tamil Nadu archaeological report’s true significance lies beyond political debates
The evidence points to extensive networks between southern, coastal, and northern regions thousands of years ago.Vaishna Roy
Gautam Bhatia’s speculative legal thriller is an honest misfire
It is rich in world-building but is weakened by burdened dialogue that seems cosmetic and unpeopled.Tanuj  Solanki
Tamil Nadu’s metallurgical mystery: The missing Copper Age
While the findings on iron use look solid, it is worth investigating how smelting knowledge first came to south India.David Killick
Tamil Nadu’s discoveries rewrite the Iron Age timeline and South India’s past
Evidence indicates iron use began as early as 3345 BCE, suggesting people and technology moved between Tamil Nadu and Harappan sites.Sharada Srinivasan
New books on the shelves
Stories about women who want to live life on their own terms, a trippy biography of the cannabis plant, and much more.
When Turquoise waters turned dark: A visual history of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands
Stunning photographs set this coffee table book apart, but historians might frown upon oversimplifications and generalisations of a colonial past.Ajay Saini
New books on the shelves
A romance in the hills featuring a 40-year-old woman, a writer-teacher’s recollections of his students in small-town Tamil Nadu, and many more.
Translated from the Malayalam by Fathima E. V.Gracy
Dreams at the river’s edge: Buland Masjid’s story
“Dreaming a Paradise” documents life in Buland Masjid, an unauthorised settlement on the banks of the Yamuna that thrives with energy and enterprise.Nipun Prabhakar
New books on the shelves
Two murder mysteries set in Goa and Delhi, a history of the great Indian middle class, and much more.
New books on the shelves
Lost in the Himalayas
Weena Pun’s debut novel ‘Kanchhi’ weaves a haunting tale of loss and resilience in rural Nepal.Varsha  Tiwary
New books on the shelves
A feminist retelling of a story from the Mahabharata, the memoir of a Sri Lankan Tamil transwoman, and much more.
The invisible she: How working-class women bear the weight of India’s economy
Neha Dixit’s The Many Lives of Syeda X is uniquely authentic and eye-opening in portraying womenfolk in big South Asian cities.Zakia Soman
New books on the shelves
A Japanese novel about feline charisma, an intellectual history of modern Muslim poets and thinkers, and much more.
A distinctive female perspective on Indo-Pak relations
Ruchi Ghanashyam’s memoir peels back the curtain on Pakistani society through her years in Islamabad, scanning both mundane and historical events.Dammu  Ravi
New books on the shelves
A Japanese cat book classic in translation, a treatise on shudra nationalism in Indian history, and much more.
Safdar Hashmi’s transformative approach to theatre as a democratic force
Through plays like Hatyare and Aurat, Hashmi tackled communalism and gender issues while building an inclusive, collaborative artistic practice.A.  Mangai
Korea through Han Kang’s The Vegetarian
Han’s novel critiques Korean society and the violence that lies beneath its surface. This year’s Nobel Prize honours her statement of resistance.Geeta  Doctor
Death at a discount
In Baburao Bagul’s collection of gut-wrenching stories, Mumbai’s graveyards, brothels, and slums form the backdrop to a theatre of desperate poverty.Janhavi Acharekar
Mir in Americanese
Ranjit Hoskote’s translations of Mir’s poetry feel hurried and stiff and do not cohere as a collection.Amitabha Bagchi
New books on the shelves
A genre-bending novel translated from Malayalam, a first-hand account of Kashmir’s geopolitics translated from Hindi, and much more.
New books on the shelves
The concluding part of the Chandal Jibon trilogy, a whirlwind romance, a survivor’s ordeal of the 2002 Gujarat riots, and more.
Unpacking the India-China yin-yang
Shastri Ramachandaran’s timely book Beyond Binaries opens a window into the complex bilateral relations between India and China.Raviprasad  Narayanan
The keeper of desolation: A Hindi story in translation
Translated from the Hindi by Sayari DebnathChandan Pandey
Modi’s ‘second republic’: Dismantling India’s democratic legacy
This is, at its core, a historian’s book. It focusses on state institutions and the rights citizenship should guarantee in a healthy democracy.Satish  Deshpande
The mango: India’s beloved fruit has a storied past
Sopan Joshi’s tribute to the mango leads the reader to an elaborate feast of history, mythology, culture, ecology, international diplomacy, and more.Nandini  Bhatia
New books on the shelves
A science fiction novel about the meaning of humanity, a book on the power of public protest, and many more.
Hari Kunzru’s artistic anxiety
Kunzru’s latest novel attempts to tackle privilege and politics in the art world, but struggles with stereotypes and unfocussed storytelling.Dakshayini  Suresh
New books on the shelves
An exploration of identity and belonging in fiction, the interface of religion, politics, and gender in non-fiction, and many more.
Ruchir Sharma’s analysis of capitalism’s flaws overlooks political solutions
While Sharma’s economic diagnosis is sharp, he misses the mark by ignoring the political dimensions necessary to resolve capitalism’s global crisis.Sukumar  Muralidharan
Loose skein: Rahman Abbas’ novel within a novel
On the Other Side is a tricky read, presenting itself as a work in progress. When judged as a formal experiment, it is more careless than cautious.Annie Zaidi
Can painting 64 Yoginis unlock the secrets of the Divine Feminine?
Whispers of the Unseen by S. Beena Unnikrishnan is a personal journey of discovery through art, challenging traditional ideas of spirituality and time.Bhavana  Nissima
Stories for the Anthropocene
Gigi Ganguly’s collection of speculative fiction, Biopeculiar, heightens our awareness of the climate-change-threatened natural world.Jaideep  Unudurti
Portrait of a people
Ismail Darbesh’s Talashnama offers a rare and enriching view of the life of the rural Muslim, thus reclaiming for the community its right to nuance.Shaoni  Sarkar
The desi hands that shaped India’s foreign policy
Nehru’s First Recruits brings to life the professional contributions, personalities, and fascinating aspects of India’s first diplomats.Vivek Katju
The rise of the Hindu Right
Siddhartha Deb’s Twilight Prisoners outlines the contribution of the liberal elite to the emergence of the Narendra Modi regime.Shakir Mir
Perfume: A Tamil story in translation
Translated from the Tamil by the author.Ashokamitran
Memory and desire
Marathi playwright Mahesh Elkunchwar’s collection of essays, The Necropolis Trilogy, conceived as a monologue with the self, is hauntingly beautiful.Deepa  Ganesh
Sequel with a soul
If homesickness was the top note in Colm Tóibín’s Brooklyn, in this follow-up novel, Long Island, we see how the homecoming unspools.Bhavya Dore
Nandita Haksar’s The Colours of Nationalism reminds us of a gentler past
The memoir nostalgically reflects on a simpler India where justice and the rights of the poor and politically disenfranchised held great significance.Kalpana Sharma
Dark nights in Puducherry
Ari Gautier brings out the seamy side of this seemingly pretty tourist town in his collection Nocturne Pondicherry.Geeta  Doctor
A daring but divisive experiment in fiction
Rachel Cusk pushes boundaries in her latest novel, blurring lines between art and life. Not recommended for the faint hearted.Rahul  Singh
Erpenbeck’s excavation of East German memory
Jenny Erpenbeck’s Kairos digs into East German history through a suffocating May-December romance, mirroring national struggles in personal turmoil.Milind  Brahme
I do not think AI is a threat to human creativity: Meru Gokhale
Former editor-in-chief at Penguin Random House says her AI-assisted editing tool Editrix makes quality book editing accessible and affordable.Kanika  Sharma
New books on the shelves
A suspenseful novel in the cli-fi genre, the much-awaited biography of Kamal Haasan, and much more.
The die is caste
Ilangovan Rajasekaran looks at the ways caste operates in Tamil Nadu through accounts of inter-caste marriages that ended in “honour killings”.V.  Arasu
The Remains of the Body: Sexuality’s ambiguous terrain through immigrant scholars’ lives
Saikat Majumdar scans the relationships among Indian immigrants in America,  focussing on the oft-neglected bisexual experiences.Shad  Naved
Paradise in flames: Rana Safvi’s novel resurrects Delhi’s doomed glory
A “Firestorm in Paradise” weaves history and fiction to paint a vivid portrait of 1857 Delhi.Madhulika  Liddle
‘Gangster’: A Marathi story in translation
Translated by Jerry Pinto.Baburao  Bagul
Broken Promises breaks little new ground in chronicling Bihar’s troubled 1990s
Mrityunjay Sharma’s book on Bihar under Lalu Prasad documents a dark era, but falls short on analysis and rigour.Supriy  Ranjan
Julia Hauser’s plague chronicle fascinates but falls short on promised insights into pandemics’ societal toll
The Moral Contagion follows the strategy of complementing facts about the bubonic plague with fictionalised elements and creative gestures.Anil  Menon
The Modi model
Christophe Jaffrelot explains the authoritarian model of governance Modi had formulated as Chief Minister, which he later implemented at the Centre.Shakir Mir
A compelling case for regulating cryptocurrency
Mitali Mukherjee’s book, while repetitive and tedious, explains the need for standardised regulations and practices for this porous currency.Eshwar Sundaresan
Caracal: The mystery cat
A redolently illustrated, rigorously scientific, and richly historical book tells the story of one of India’s most elusive cats.Divya  Gandhi
Civil servants as change-makers
Based on years of fieldwork, Akshay Mangla’s book shows how bureaucrats influence outcomes by enabling community participation and local ownership.Uma  Mahadevan-Dasgupta
Swadeshi Steam: A shipping company propelled by patriotism
The story of Tamil freedom fighter V.O. Chidambaram Pillai, who helmed a shipping company that hoped to stand up to a British hegemon at sea.Manu S. Pillai
Clues, culprits, and cultural crossovers: Indian detective fiction is entering the mainstream
Detective fiction writers now weave captivating tales that blend mystery, social commentary, and local flavours into page-turning bestsellers.Tarun  K. Saint
New books on the shelves
Fiction about love, community, and politics, essays about the shrinking of space for free speech in India post-2014, and much more.
Moments in the sands of time
Yuvan Aves’ book is a blend of a naturalist’s journal, a coming-of-age memoir, a treatise on meditation and mindfulness, and much more.Mohit M.  Rao
Death, a user’s manual
Minakshi Dewan’s exemplary book maps the changing ritual and material aspects of funerals in contemporary India.Ravi Nandan  Singh
K.G. Subramanyan (1924-2016): Artist, activist, provocateur, teacher
An exhibition celebrating Subramanyan’s centenary asks us to critically engage with his legacy to reaffirm his place among India’s greatest artists.Soumitra Das
New books on the shelves
A forgotten Magahi novel in English translation, an archival history of Kerala’s Gulf migrants, and much more.
Captain America
Superbly written and argued, Tom Stevenson’s Someone Else’s Empire reads like a primer on American imperial hegemony and its deadly consequences.Susan Ram
The unique memoirs of Mughal princess Gulbadan Begum
As Mughal history unfolds across the reigns of Babar and Humayun, Ruby Lal’s Vagabond Princess tells the story of the womenfolk of this dynasty.Madhulika  Liddle
The saint, the song, and the social revolution
A new music project titled Anbenum Peruveli radically reinterprets the work of the 19th-century Tamil poet-prophet in the year of his bicentenary.Abhirami Girija Sriram
Viral fever: The curious spectacle of campaign films
Potent tools for disseminating political ideologies and pushing stereotypes, these films offer insights into elections as well as everyday realities.S.V. Srinivas
Movie trailers: The unofficial manifestos of our time
Endlessly shared till its beliefs pass off as the truth, the film trailer mirrors our world: fact and fiction, blurred by repetition.Lawrence Liang
Tastes of the earth
Disarmingly honest, Food Journeys is very different from the gushing food books that come out every second day.Anusua Mukherjee
New books on the shelves
Fiction about the idea of belonging and exile, non fiction about the idea of Asia, and much more.
A date with Apollo
How an exhibition of sculptures at Mumbai’s CSMVS allows one to look at antique art not from the usual vantage point of Europe but of India.Vishakha N.   Desai
A Rashomon-like social commentary on conservatism and misogyny
Equal parts whodunnit and social drama, Atharva Pandit’s Hurda marks the arrival of a sincere, diligent new voice in Indian English literature.Aditya Mani   Jha

Environment.text: Meet the ecowarriors of today
From reviving rainforests to protecting the fauna, India’s modern ecologists are advancing evidence-based conservation and promoting community action.Anusua Mukherjee
Mending Manas
Once ravaged by Assam’s 1980s insurgency, Manas National Park revived through the grit and stewardship of its local communities.Rajkamal  Goswami
The snow leopard’s village
How members of a Himalayan community in Spiti have woven their lives around the snow leopard, creating a blueprint for human-carnivore coexistence.Megha  Moorthy
Conservation as coexistence
Conserving nature is not about fencing forests but about sustaining the knowledge, culture, and land-use systems that bind people and nature together.Rajkamal  Goswami
Birds of honour
Once shunned as “dirty”, the greater adjutant is thriving again, thanks to conservationists in Bihar and Assam.Asad  Rahmani
The hidden costs of ethanol blending
Telangana and Andhra Pradesh face the fallout as blending goals override concerns about water scarcity, pollution, and rural livelihoods.Ayesha  Minhaz
Whose future?
On Telangana’s fertile edge, “Future City” promises growth—but for local communities, it begins with loss of livelihood and habitat destruction.Ayesha  Minhaz
Why all of Kerala says no to deep-sea mining
Cutting across party lines, Kerala opposes the new law on deep-sea mining, fearing it will ruin marine biodiversity and  fishing livelihoods.Ayaskant  Das
Failed by the judiciary
Tamil Nadu’s courts keep issuing orders against illegal mining. The quarries stay open. The activists get killed. And justice? Mostly adjourned.EDGAR  KAISER,HENRI  TIPHAGNE
Mines that rule: How Tamil Nadu’s quarries dug themselves above the law
An undisclosed Tamil Nadu government report on illegal quarries in just one block of one district reveals the scale—and the scare—of the trade.Ilangovan Rajasekaran
What lies beneath: The real cost of Tamil Nadu’s illegal quarries
They poison wells, ruin farms, and drive out wildlife—but illegal quarries still thrive, chipping away at law, land, and livelihood.R.S.A.  Mughilan
Killed on civic duty
In Tamil Nadu, opposing illegal mining can be fatal. Most murders are buried as accidents, while the mafia thrives on political backing.Ilangovan Rajasekaran
The sixth mass extinction is here—and it’s killing humans too
India’s vanishing wildlife is no longer just a biodiversity crisis—it is a public health emergency born of human-induced ecological collapse.Divya  Gandhi
Indian grey wolf: Persecuted guardian of the grasslands
Rarer than tigers, the endangered predator is nearly forgotten. Once killed as vermin, its biggest threat now is hybridisation with domestic dogs.Mihir  Godbole,Abi Tamim Vanak
Editor’s Note: A sanctuary for the few, a graveyard for the many
Public habitats are vanishing, while private ventures flaunt luxurious animal care. Meanwhile, the state retreats from ecological responsibility.Vaishna Roy
An obit for Patai Takaru
Great Nicobar’s mitigation plans reduce restoration to tree-planting and reef relocation—a deceptive fix for irreversible ecological loss.Rohan   Arthur,T. R. Shankar   Raman
The Great Nicobar Project: A costly miscalculation?
The proposed container port lacks commercial logic, its naval merits are unclear—so why is this project being pushed through at any cost?M. Rajshekhar
Vedanta’s oil exploration plan threatens Assam’s last gibbon sanctuary
Modi government’s new policy lets Cairn Oil & Gas advance Rs.264 crore project near Hollongapar reserve, while wildlife board demands inspection.Ayaskant  Das
Fishing in the photovoltaic waters of Nathsagar
A solar plant in Paithan’s reservoir, where fishers eke out a meagre living, could upset the delicate ecosystem and snuff out traditional communities.Pragathi  Ravi,Mitul Kajaria
Editor’s Note: We need a bigger, better heat action plan
As heatwaves intensify, India must scale up efforts to protect its vulnerable, boost climate resilience, and accelerate the shift to clean energy.Vaishna Roy
Kashmir’s famed saffron fields are under siege
The fields are weathering everything from a prolonged dry spell to rodents, and cement dust to the influx of Iranian imports.Auqib  Javeed
‘Not amrit kaal for tiger science in India’: K. Ullas Karanth
The tiger conservation expert stresses the need for scientific management of tiger reserves and discusses the increasing tiger-human conflict.Vikhar Ahmed Sayeed

Economy.text: India’s growth dream rests more on political spin and statistical sleight-of-hand than on real economic fundamentals.Ashoka Mody
Time to say no to neoliberal orthodoxy
India’s export-led growth fuels a divide: high-productivity services with few jobs vs. stagnant manufacturing and wage gaps. A reboot is needed.Srinivas Raghavendra
The clock is ticking on India’s demographic dividend
Without job creation and deep reforms, a once-in-a-century opportunity may become a long-term burden.Ajay  Chhibber
Understanding Gen Z, the generation without history
Raised in post-liberalisation era, they are hyperconnected yet alienated. They seek value in a world of information overload and economic instability.Rama  Bijapurkar,Mathangi  Krishnamurthy

Science & Technology.text: Spooky action at the Hadron Collider; the milk of human kindness; and a cosmic goliath
LHC observes top quark entanglement; breastfeeding safe post-breast cancer; and astronomers spot largest pair of black hole jets.R.  Ramachandran
A decades-old virus resurges; Uncle Sam’s chip choke on China; and light-charged supercapacitors to spark an energy revolution
Chandipura virus claims 82 lives in India; US tightens tech exports to China; and IISc scientists unveil light-powered energy storage.R.  Ramachandran
Omega Centauri’s black hole, record-energy neutrino, and GaN semiconductors for nuclear reactors
Evidence for central black hole in Omega Centauri; undersea telescope spots most energetic neutrino; GaN semiconductors to monitor nuclear reactors.R.  Ramachandran
CERN’s funds crunch, new tech for nuclear fuel, and airborne pathogens get a new name
A new particle collider needs funds, a more efficient way to process irradiated nuclear fuel, and agreement on a common term for pathogens.R.  Ramachandran
A 400-year-old solar mystery, mass-producing recombinant proteins, and safer X-rays
Solving a puzzle Galileo struggled with, using MSG and ethanol to produce recombinant proteins, and a step closer to gentler medical imaging.R.  Ramachandran
Why 2023 was the hottest summer yet, and a way to improve lithium-ion batteries
With the help of tree-ring data, scientists have found summer 2023 was the hottest thus far. Researchers have developed a new electrolyte that improves lithium-ion batteries.R.  Ramachandran
Vampire bacteria, Tarapur reactor renewal, and uncovering Fermi’s exotic particle prophecy
How some bacteria are attracted to blood; why scientists are extending life of Tarapur nuclear plant; and how a 1949 prediction is proven true now.R.  Ramachandran
Cholesterol-chomping gut bacteria can reduce risk of heart attack
The Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument experiment has provided the largest and the most precise 3D map of the universe to date.R.  Ramachandran
While you are asleep, your brain flushes out waste
Cash-strapped NASA may gradually shut down Chandra Observatory and researchers developed a device which produces high-intensity radiation.R.  Ramachandran
Powerful magnetic field leaves imprint on nuclear matter
Also, a synthetic human antibody takes us closer to a solution against snake venoms, and using light to tackle antibiotic resistance in bacteria.R.  Ramachandran
